attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of right heel pain. The pain is most severe with her first steps in the morning and after prolonged sitting. She describes the pain as sharp and localised to the bottom of her heel. She works as a retail assistant, standing for eight-hour shifts. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity of the right foot. Pain is reproduced with passive dorsiflexion of the toes. There is no swelling, erythema, or warmth. The remainder of the musculoskeletal examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic symptoms of plantar fasciitis, characterised by sharp heel pain that is worst with the first few steps in the morning and after periods of prolonged sitting. The physical examination finding of point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity (where the plantar fascia inserts into the calcaneus) further supports this diagnosis.

Stretching exercises are recommended as first-line treatment for plantar fasciitis, based on quality evidence (NHMRC Level 2 evidence). These exercises involve the patient sitting down, crossing the affected leg over the unaffected leg, and pulling the toes back toward the shin for 10 seconds to stretch the plantar fascia. This should be performed with 10 repetitions at least three times daily, particularly before taking the first step in the morning and after prolonged sitting. These stretching exercises can significantly reduce pain and improve function in patients with chronic plantar fasciitis. A comprehensive approach should also include strengthening of the foot intrinsic muscles, calf muscles, and the entire kinetic chain. Low height isometric heel raises with sustained holds can be particularly beneficial.

Corticosteroid injection into the plantar fascia may provide short-term pain relief but carries risks including plantar fascia rupture, fat pad atrophy, and infection. It is generally reserved for patients who have failed conservative measures including stretching exercises, and is not appropriate as first-line management.

Custom-made orthotic devices may be helpful for some patients with plantar fasciitis, particularly those with biomechanical abnormalities such as excessive pronation. However, they are more expensive than stretching exercises and should be considered as an adjunctive treatment rather than first-line therapy. That said, taping and prefabricated orthotics may offer symptomatic relief and can be used alongside stretching and strengthening exercises.

Night splints maintain the ankle in a neutral position during sleep, preventing the plantar fascia from contracting overnight. While they can be effective, they are often poorly tolerated due to discomfort and sleep disruption. They are typically considered when simpler interventions like stretching exercises have failed.

Extracorporeal shock wave therapy involves delivering shock waves to the affected area to stimulate healing. It is generally considered for recalcitrant cases that have not responded to other conservative measures for at least six months. It is not appropriate as first-line management and may be associated with pain during treatment and temporary worsening of symptoms.

Other conservative measures that may be considered alongside stretching exercises include activity modification, appropriate footwear, ice application, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for pain relief. The majority of patients with plantar fasciitis will improve with conservative management within 6-12 months.

It's worth noting that while Achilles tendon stretching may provide additional benefit, it should only be performed after the plantar fascia stretch to avoid micro-tearing and inflammation associated with plantar fasciitis.
learningPoints: Plantar fasciitis is characterised by intense heel pain with the first few steps in the morning or after periods of non-weight bearing.
Plantar fascia stretching exercises are an effective first-line treatment for chronic plantar fasciitis.
A comprehensive approach to plantar fasciitis includes stretching, strengthening of foot intrinsics and calf muscles, and may include taping or orthotics for symptom relief.
references: RACGP Handbook of Non-Drug Interventions:  Stretching exercises for plantar fasciitis. First published: October 2014. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/handi/a-z/s/stretching-exercises-for-plantar-fasciitis

Rio E, Mayes S, Cook J. Heel pain: a practical approach. Aust Fam Physician. 2015 Mar;44(3):96-101. PMID: 25770572. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2015/march/heel-pain-a-practical-approach
